segments.text: Now I come to another Sutta, 2.4.6.
Once the Exalted One was staying near Sabati at Jaita Grove in Anata Pindika's Pa.
Now great number of devas of tranquil mind came to visit the Exalted One and on coming
to him saluted the Exalted One and stood at one side.
So standing those devas said this to the Exalted One.
Here in this Venables, here is this Venables Sariputta at Espa in the Terrace House of Migara's mother,
that means Visaka, teaching the monks about the person who is fatted inwardly and the person fatted
outwardly. The company is delighted. It was a good thing, Lord, if the Exalted One would pay
a visit to the Venables Sariputta out of compassion for him. The Exalted One consented by silence.
Then even as a strong man might straighten out his bent arm or drawing his arm stretched out.
Even so did the Exalted One vanish from Jaita Grove and appear in the presence of the Venables Sariputta
at Espa in the Terrace House of Migara's mother. And the Venables Sariputta saluted the Exalted One
and sat down at one side. As it thus said, the Exalted One said this to the Venables Sariputta.
Sariputta, a great number of devas of tranquil mind came to visit me and he repeated the request
that is they asked Buddha to visit the Venables Sariputta. Then the Buddha continued.
Now Sariputta, those devas, though numbering 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 or 60,
yet they all stood in a space not greater than that made by the point of a gimler
and that without crowding each other. Now you may think Sariputta, surely it was yonder.
That means in another world that those devas' minds must have been trained to this attainment.
Namely, that though numbering 10, 20, 30, up to 60, yet they all stood in a space no greater than
that made by the point of a gimler and that without crowding each other. But that is not how you
must regard it Sariputta. It was just here a Sariputta that the mind was trained to this attainment.
Therefore Sariputta thus must be trained yourself. We will become tranquil in senses and tranquil in
mind. That is how you must train yourself. Indeed Sariputta, those who are thus tranquil in sense,
tranquil in mind, their bodily action also will be tranquil and the same for speech and thought.
Thus must be trained yourself. We will present to our fellows in the holy life,
tranquility of speech and tranquility of thought, a present of tranquility. That is how you must
train yourself. Those wonders of other views Sariputta who have not heard this Dharma teaching
utterly discomputed as the end of the Sutta. Here in this Sutta, the Buddha is saying that
these devas have only beings, 60 of them, stood in a space no greater than that made by the point
of a gimler. That means the point of a needle, all of them could stand at the point of a needle,
such a small space and without crowding each other. The Buddha is saying that they did not train
their mind to do this in the heaven world or somewhere. It was just here in the human plane.
So this human plane we can see here is a very important plane because here we create a lot
of karma. Whereas in the other planes, we are reaping the results of our karma mainly.
If we go to the heavens, you find that the devas, they enjoy themselves so much and they are not
thinking of cultivating themselves, the only thing of enjoying themselves and that is quite natural
in such a place. Then the beings in the lower planes, in the woeful planes, they are just
reaping the bad fruit of the bad karma. So they don't create so much new karma as the human
plane. That's why the human plane, which is a very, very good plane for us to be because there's
a place where we cultivate ourselves. So even for this, if we have strength of mind, and then next
time when we become devas, we might be able to do that. Now we come to 2.5.3 Buddha said monks,
there are these two companies. What two, the distinguished and the ignoble and what monks
is the ignoble company? Here in monks, in whatsoever company, the monks are luxurious,
laks taking the lead in backsliding, shirking the duty of seclusion, and make no effort to
reach the unattain to win the goal not one, to realize the unrealized. The generation that
follows comes to follow along with their view. That generation also is luxurious. Laks taking
the lead in backsliding, shirking the duty of seclusion, and make no effort to reach the unattain
to win the goal not one, to realize the unrealized. This company, monks, is called the ignoble.
And what monks is the distinguished company? Here in monks, in whatsoever company, the senior
monks are not luxurious, not laks, not taking the lead in backsliding, not shirking the duty of
seclusion, and they make an effort to reach the unattain to win the goal not one, to realize the
unrealized. The generation that follows them comes to follow along with their view. That generation
also is not luxurious, not laks, etc., and make an effort to realize the unrealized. This company,
monks, is called the distinguished, these are the two companies, and of these two, the distinguished
company has the pre-eminent. The Buddha here, you see, the Buddha is talking about
companies of monks. He is not talking about a single monk, because during the Buddha's time,
monks used to group together in big numbers, and they would travel from one monastery to another.
They would not stay long in one place, because they are supposed to be homeless, and they go from one
place to another. And so, the Buddha praised those monks, who practiced to win the goal of the
spiritual life. Two point five, point six monks, there are these two companies. What do the company
train in blaster? Here, speaking means, speaking empty arrogant words, not in discussion by inquiry,
and the company trained in discussion by inquiry, not in speaking empty arrogant words.
And what monks is the company trained in speaking empty arrogant words, not inquiry? Here in
monks, in whatsoever company, the monks listen not to the discourses uttered by the Tata Gata,
discourses profound, deep in meaning, transcending the world, dealing with emptiness when they are
recited. They lend not a ready ear to them, apply not to them a mind bent on understanding,
consider not that those teachings are something to be learned by heart and master. But when those
discourses made by poets, tricked out with the fair sounding phrases, discourses external to dharma,
utterances of disciples. When such are recited, they listen there to lend a ready ear to them.
Apply to them a mind bent on understanding and consider that those teachings are something
to be learned by heart and master. And when they are master that teaching, they do not question
each other about it, do not open up a discussion thus. What is this? What is the meaning of this?
When they neither open up the unrevealed, nor explained, the unexplained, nor dispelled doubts on
diverse doubtful points of dharma, such a company monks is called trained in speaking empty arrogant
words, not in inquiry. And what monks is the company trained in inquiry, not trained in speaking
empty arrogant words? Here in monks, in whatsoever company the monks listen not to those discourses
made by poets, tricked out with fair sounding phrases, discourses external to dharma, utterances of
disciples. But listen to those uttered by the Tata Gata, discourses profound, deep in meaning,
transcending the world, dealing with emptiness and having mastered that question each other about it,
open up discussion thus. What is this? What is the meaning of this? When such open up the
unrevealed, explain the unexplained and dispelled doubts on diverse doubtful points of dharma,
such a company is called trained in inquiry, not in speaking empty arrogant words. These are the
two companies, and of these two the letter has the pre-eminence. Here again the Buddha is talking
about two companies of monks, one is the monks who listen to the discourses of the Buddha,
like the sutras here, like what you all are doing today, listen to the Buddha's words,
whereas the second company, those who listen to the utterances of disciples, what are these discourses
that are the utterances of disciples? Attrances of disciples are meant to be the teachings of later monks
because the disciples here refers to the monk disciples of the Buddha. The Buddha could see that
in the future the later monks would write some new books and all that, so there are some people
instead of studying the Buddha's discourses, they prefer to study the words of these other monks
and the Buddha does not encourage that. The other thing about this you find is that the Buddha
encourage us to discuss and inquire whatever teachings we come across, we should inquire and discuss
about it in the Mangala Sutta, this is called Dhamma Sakacha, a discussion of Dhamma.
In the Sangyuta Nikaya, number 20, the 20th Sangyuta, the Buddha warned that in the future people would
not want to study and master the sutras and because people are not interested in the sutras,
instead of listening to the sutras, they'd rather listen to some other teachings, then it will
cause the disappearance of the sutras. It is because the sutras are so important, that's why
we take the trouble to read out the sutras so that people can understand the real teachings of the
Buddha. Some people would be surprised to learn that there's no such word as Stripitaka or Tipitaka,
spoken by the Buddha. Tipitaka was something that was coined later. In the sutras, we find the
Buddha only mentions the teachings as Dhamma Vinaya. Dhamma, according to the Angutra Nikaya,
the Dhamma refers to the sutras, the sutras of the Buddha are called Dhamma and Vinaya are the
disciplinary rules for the monks. So, the later books you find, like the commentaries and the
Abidama, there are certain contradictions. If you take the trouble to study the sutras in detail,
then you'll find that there are certain contradictions with the Abidama, with the commentaries. It is only
the earliest four Nikayas that there are no contradictions. And these earliest four Nikayas are accepted
by everyone, including Mahayana, including Tibetan Buddhism and Theravada Buddhism as being the
original words of the Buddha. If we study the four Nikayas, we find that there are no contradictions in
them. They are very consistent. That is why, because these are not controversial books, that it is
better if we want to study to depend on the four Nikayas basically. In the Diga Nikaya number 26,
the Buddha said, be a lamb unto yourselves, be a refuge unto yourselves with no other refuge.
Take the Dhamma as your lamb, take the Dhamma as your refuge with no other refuge. So, the Buddha
is telling us that we take the Dhamma, the Buddha's teachings to be our master.
And that's why we always take refuge with the Buddha, Dhamma and Sangha. That means the
original teachings of the Buddha, the Buddha is original teachings and those Aryan disciples.
But the trouble is sometimes it's hard to tell which are the Aryan disciples. So,
it was decided in the Second Sangha Council about a hundred years after the Buddha's passing away
that if any monk teaches anything, that is consistent with the Buddha's teachings.
Then we listen to him. If they teach us anything that is inconsistent with the Buddha's teachings
and that means in the Sutta, we don't listen to them. That was decided 100 years after the Buddha's
passing away. Now we come to 2.6.12, the Buddha said, monks, when in a dispute, there is worthy
warfare on both sides with tenacity of view, melis of heart, salkiness and discontent.
Once personality is ruffled, therefore monks, it may be expected that this will conduce
to protracted bitter contentious strife and the monks will be unable to live at ease.
But when in a dispute, there is worthy warfare on both sides with tenacity of view, melis of heart,
salkiness and discontent. Once personality is not ruffled, then it may be expected that it will not
conduce to protracted bitter contentious strife and the monks will be able to live at ease.
So here, the Buddha is saying that what the Buddha is saying here, concerns monks,
is also equally applicable to lay people. They mean that if there is any worthy warfare,
that means there is any quarrel or any dispute, we should not get emotionally upset,
become emotionally ruffled. If we become emotionally ruffled or upset, then it will lead
to a long bitter strife and whereas if there is, if you are not too emotionally involved,
then that quarrel can end in not too long time. Sometimes when people practice mindfulness,
if they do not have some somebody, then when they become emotionally involved,
then you find that all the mindfulness has become lost, then no more mindful.
It is difficult to remain mindful when you become emotionally involved and emotionally upset.
So we have to always practice this upeka, this equanimity, whatever happens in life,
we try to remain equanimous. 2.10, 0.1, the Buddha said, monks, there are these two fools,
what two? He who showed us a burden that does not befall him and he who sherks a burden that
befalls him, these are the two. And then 2.10, 0.2, monks, there are these two wise ones,
what two? He who showed us a burden that befalls him and he who takes not up one that does not
befall him, these are the two. So these two suttas, the Buddha is saying that what is our duty,
what is our burden, we should take it up, what is not our burden, we should not take it up.
So if a person carries a burden that is not his burden, he is a fool or what he should,
the burden that he should carry, for example towards his family and all that he does not carry
that duty, that burden, then he is a fool again. Now he comes to 2.11, 0.2, monks,
these two persons are hard to find in the world, what two? The one who is first to do a favour
and he who is grateful for what is done, these are the two persons hard to find in the world.
That's the end of the sutta. You see this person who is first to do a favour,
few people would want to do a kind deed towards somebody, unless he owes that person a kind deed.
For a person to do a favour, first means he is willing to do without any pre-condition,
and that kind of person is rare to find. Then also a person who is grateful, very often we find that
we have done something for somebody, and later when we need a favour from him, he has forgotten that
we have been kind to him before. So these are the two persons hard to find.
Now 2.11, 0.8, monks, these two things are causes for the arising of wrong view. What two?
Another's utterance and untaraged consideration,
Yoniso Manasikara, 2.11, 0.9, monks, these two things are the causes for the arising of right
view. What two? Another's utterance and tarar attention or tarar consideration. These are the two
suites. Now this right view, if a person has right view, then he is considered to be a sutta
one who has entered the stream and area. So here it is saying that to get right view which
has to listen to another's utterance that means that another's teaching, and then you consider it
thoroughly. You pay close attention, you pay proper attention, you consider it and you might be able to
get right view. And for wrong view also, you hear and then you don't consider properly and you
might end up with wrong view, especially if you hear wrong teaching and you accept it because you
don't have tarar attention or tarar consideration. So from this sutta it appears like it is possible
and just from listening to the dhamma to attain right view or enter the stream and this seem to
have happened during the Buddha's time. Many people who came to listen to the Buddha teach the dhamma
very soon after that the Buddha said that enter the stream. Now 2.12.1, the Buddha said
the believing monk, if he would aspire perfectly, should thus aspire. May I be like unto Sariputta and
Mobileana. Mangs, these are a sort of scale, these are a sort of measure for my disciples who are
monks, namely Sariputta and Mobileana. Now the reason I'm reading this is again that there's a
contradiction with the Mahayana Sutta. And the Buddha here is saying that all his mangs disciples
should be like Sariputta and Mobileana. Sariputta is supposed to have the highest wisdom and Mobileana
is the psychic power is the best. So it is not like in the Mahayana Sutta, Sutta, we say that
Sariputta did not understand the Buddha's teachings that he had in video wisdom and all that.
